title: SPLIT Localization and Rebuild
wikitext:
[]: [SPLIT] Localization and Rebuild             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Mon Oct 17, 2011 7:38 pm Split of this thread .~ SSD icomeanon4 wrote: 4) Would it have been *so* bad? One word: YEEEEEEESSSS. English does not use Japanese honorifics. Translations are not meant to assist in learning aspects of the other language; that's what watching it in the other language is for. Including honorifics in a dub would wreck the goal of translating: to carry the intent of the original language into the language of the audience as fully as possible, and in a way that assumes no prior knowledge of the original language. Besides, we have ways in English of doing the exact same thing that honorifics do. For example, if you call a police officer "man" or "dude" as opposed to "Mr." or "Sir," you're probably being disrespectful. Literal translation does not preserve the original intent; it actually obfuscates it because the exact wording doesn't do the same thing in the new language as it did in the old. And besides, those examples you gave would not sound better in English. Not by a long shot.

[]:             Quote Post by John » Mon Oct 17, 2011 9:27 pm I agree about not adding honorifics. Because they are specifically made to be used in their country of origin. Unless these honorifics are to be expressed for something really important on dialogue (obviously like a sudden language change between characters/scenes), then they only confuse the non-multilingual audience. Each language stays with it's own words, and that's for the best.

[]:             Quote Post by qu4d » Tue Oct 18, 2011 4:33 am The german dub has those honorifics. I kinda like it ^__^

[]:             Quote Post by chaosakita » Tue Oct 18, 2011 12:19 pm If you want honorifics, you can just watch the Japanese version. I'm pretty sure it'd be included with whatever English release you could watch. As for me, I do not like the English voices at all, so this has never been an issue for me at all.

[]:             Quote Post by Jayfive » Tue Oct 18, 2011 12:44 pm If you want honorifics, you can just watch the Japanese version.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Tue Oct 18, 2011 12:50 pm It's good to learn the honorifics, so that way if you pick them up in the Japanese dub, you know what it is their talking about. But I agree with not using them in the sub translations. Though, if they don't replace them with similar-meaning English words like "Dude", "Sir", "Miss", "Kid", "Cutie", "Sweetie", ect., then they might as well leave Japanese honorifics in the sub/dub. I'd rather people get confused and then learn them rather than having these subtexts completely pass them by. And about that whole sequel thing, I'm sure we'll know without a shadow of a doubt by the end of "FINAL" if this is a sequel or not. Until then, there's no reason to treat it like a sequel.

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Tue Oct 18, 2011 4:49 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: Though, if they don't replace them with similar-meaning English words like "Dude", "Sir", "Miss", "Kid", "Cutie", "Sweetie", ect., then they might as well leave Japanese honorifics in the sub/dub. I'd rather people get confused and then learn them rather than having these subtexts completely pass them by.

[]:             Quote Post by Jayfive » Wed Oct 19, 2011 7:33 am Indeed, it is the true spirit of Eva that we are left with more questions than answers

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Wed Oct 19, 2011 7:49 am I would think "sweetie" can be the considered a comfortable equivalent to "-chan", though it usually only sounds its most natural if an adult/older female says it. ("Hey, Yotsuba-chan!" "Hey, sweetie!") But yeah, I agree with the most part that the sense of Japanese honorifics can be achieved better through contact. Like you said, America is a larger country than Japan, and therefore tends to have more varieties of localized dialogue and speech patters, making a translators job harder for national releases. In most cases "One-san"/"Ne-chan"/"Ne-san" can be easily translated into "sis" (short for the English word "sister"), but "Oni-san"/"Ni-chan"/"Ni-san" can't be translated into "bro" (short for "brother") without the characters sounding all "gangstah" to some Americans ("gangstah" = "gangter": being part of a tough gang and involved in crime, or imitating those who are), which would most certainly be the wrong approach most of the time. Sometimes it's just best to leave it up to wording, performance, and context. gatotsu911 wrote: So optimistic!

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Wed Oct 19, 2011 1:45 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: I would think "sweetie" can be the considered a comfortable equivalent to "-chan", though it usually only sounds its most natural if an adult/older female says it. ("Hey, Yotsuba-chan!" "Hey, sweetie!") But yeah, I agree with the most part that the sense of Japanese honorifics can be achieved better through contact. Like you said, America is a larger country than Japan, and therefore tends to have more varieties of localized dialogue and speech patters, making a translators job harder for national releases. In most cases "One-san"/"Ne-chan"/"Ne-san" can be easily translated into "sis" (short for the English word "sister"), but "Oni-san"/"Ni-chan"/"Ni-san" can't be translated into "bro" (short for "brother") without the characters sounding all "gangstah" to some Americans ("gangstah" = "gangter": being part of a tough gang and involved in crime, or imitating those who are), which would most certainly be the wrong approach most of the time. Sometimes it's just best to leave it up to wording, performance, and context.

[]:             Quote Post by Tarnsman » Wed Oct 19, 2011 4:23 pm Honorifics in dubbing

[]:             Quote Post by Jayfive » Thu Oct 20, 2011 6:11 am Thats for the replies and all , but any chance of an answer to question 3? Is it something in the language Shinji uses - i.e. something a Japanese person would find shocking that son would say to his father - or just the fact hes making a stand at all? In the english dub if I recall, he simply shouts 'Father!' and Gendo gasps, which tbh sounds a bit daft.

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Thu Oct 20, 2011 3:54 pm Tarnsman wrote: "Man I'm sure enjoying watching this English dub of a Japanese production... but you know, I wish there was someway it could be more Japanese."

[]:             Quote Post by Azathoth » Thu Oct 20, 2011 4:09 pm gatotsu911 wrote: Is there a formal vs. casual way of addressing one's father - e.g. "father" vs. "dad"?

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Thu Oct 20, 2011 6:58 pm Tarnsman wrote: "Man I'm sure enjoying watching this English dub of a Japanese production... but you know, I wish there was someway it could be more Japanese."

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Thu Oct 20, 2011 8:46 pm @Azathoth: Thanks for the clarification. I guess that would explain the somewhat controversial translation choice. I guess it's excusable, since "dad" does not necessarily connote a close relationship (i.e. plenty of people with poor relationships with their fathers might still call them "dad" simply because that is a standard form of address outside of explicitly formal contexts). Though I agree that rendering it as "father" still makes more sense and effectively conveys the coldness and distance between Shinji and Gendo. @FFFFE: The Paprika dub sucked (all I remember about it is that the voice of Chiba/Paprika was terrible, I had forgotten about the honorifics), but some of the other Sony dubs I've seen (e.g. Tekkon Kinkreet and The Sky Crawlers ) have actually been pretty damn good. If random honorifics and Japanese words inserted into English dialogue for no good reason drive you up the wall, then for the love of god don't ever watch FLCL dubbed.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Thu Oct 20, 2011 10:55 pm gatotsu911 wrote: If random honorifics and Japanese words inserted into English dialogue for no good reason drive you up the wall, then for the love of god don't ever watch FLCL dubbed.

[]:             Quote Post by Rodeo » Fri Oct 21, 2011 10:12 am FLCL probably has the best anime dub I've ever heard despite it keeping some Japanese honorifics. I guess the whole show was so quirky, bizarre and off-beat that it just kind of worked. Like I said, honorifics can be appropriate if you want the dialog to have an exotic flavor, but more often than not, this has nothing to do with what the original authors were trying to convey and thus gets in the way.

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Fri Oct 21, 2011 1:29 pm @Monk Ed: That's probably the most legitimate argument I've heard in favor of retaining honorifics, but I'm still against it. How much "de-localization" is too much? Should anime dubs have the actors attempting Japanese accents, to be "authentic"? If the work being dubbed was intended to feel culturally familiar to its target audience, doesn't retaining foreign words - thus making it feel alien and exotic - betray authorial intent? The way I see it, the characters are either speaking English or Japanese - if they are speaking English, then as Tarnsman says, what on earth is the point of trying to make their speech "more Japanese"? You can't get much more Japanese than having them speak Japanese , and presumably if you want to get the "culturally authentic" experience than you are watching in Japanese to begin with. If they are speaking English, let them speak English and sound like native English-speakers, because the entire point of a translation and particularly dub is to make a product accessible and immersive for people of the target language. Having the characters speak English peppered with foreign words paints a completely different picture of the dialogue, and while that may work in a setting wherein the point is to make the audience go "oooooooh, that's foreign !!" (e.g. Assassin's Creed ), if that is not the authorial intent then doing so creates a messy compromise that will satisfy neither purists nor people who are watching in their own language to hear it in their own language . Also, I abhor the FLCL dub in general because the ADR director (and Weeaboo Supreme) Marc Handler made the horrid decision of forcing each of the voice actors to meticulously mimic the inflections of their Japanese counterparts while reading their dialogue in English. NO, THAT IS BAD, THAT IS SO BAD, THAT IS NOT HOW LOCALIZATION WORKS, JESUS CHRIST. The tonal inflections of the Japanese and English languages are nothing alike, so the result of this is that the characters sound like autistic aliens learning to speak English for the first time. People say that it "fits in with the tone of the show", except it really doesn't, because while maybe they were supposed to sound strange and quirky in Japanese, they were most definitely also supposed to sound like actual human beings that the audience could relate to . It is literally beyond me how anyone who speaks English as a native language can actually like the dub of FLCL , let alone consider it better than the original Japanese of which it is, of its own accord, merely a shallow mimicry, because it is kind of an insult to both languages. I can't sit through more than 5 minutes of that shit, I've tried. Oh, and don't get me started on "Jennifer Sekiguchi" (who, as I've recently learned, is actually Stephanie Sheh, thus affirming for me years of disliking Sheh's voiceover work) whose atonal whine as Mamimi fills me with an overwhelming impulse to kick my screen and jab sharp objects in my ears every second I hear it. [/rant]

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Fri Oct 21, 2011 3:36 pm Unless I read the opening post wrong, isn't this thread about more then dub decisions? Probably best to focus on that (Shinji's personality, beast mode, whatever else) too, since there are plenty of threads to discuss dub choices, especially in this subforum.
